source_metadata.title_raw: Jeffrey A. Walter - A- 19-126 - June 24, 2019 - Sonoma

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 24, 2019

Jeffrey A. Walter
City Attorney
670 W. Napa St. Suite F
Sonoma, CA 95476

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-126

Dear Mr. Walter:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Sonoma City Mayor and Councilmember Amy Harrington regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does Mayor Amy Harrington's interest in her residential real property, rental office space, and law practice, preclude her from participating in or influencing governmental decision-making pertinent to the proposed zoning ordinance to allow for a limited number of commercial cannabis businesses in the commercial or mixed use zones?

CONCLUSION

No. It is not reasonably foreseeable the proposed zoning ordinance decision before the City Council will have a material financial effect on Mayor Harrington's residential real property or law practice. Because Mayor Harrington rents her office on a month-to-month basis, she does not have a financial interest in this property under the Act.

1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-126
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City of Sonoma is a general law city located in the County of Sonoma. The City is
approximately two square miles in area. Its population is estimated to be above 11,000. Except for
deliveries of medicinal cannabis products from licensed businesses located outside of the City's
boundaries, all other adult-use and medicinal commercial cannabis activities are currently
prohibited in all zones throughout the City.

The City Council will soon consider the adoption of a proposed ordinance ("ordinance")
amending the City's zoning code (in Sonoma, called the "development code") to allow no more
than four, specified commercial cannabis businesses to locate in commercial or mixed use zones in
the City; provided that such businesses first obtain, among other things, a use permit. None of the
commercial cannabis businesses permitted under the proposed amendment will be permitted as of
right, and must go through an extensive application process which includes an internal city
committee, approval by the City Council, and approval of a City cannabis business permit.

The proposed ordinance allows no more than one store-front retail dispensary to be located
only in the City's Commercial (C) zoning district. It would allow for one non-store front retail
dispensary, one testing laboratory and one non-volatile manufacturing business limited to activities
permitted under a State License Type N or P to be conditionally permitted in the C zoning district
and the Mixed Use (MX) zoning district. The proposed ordinance identifies four "buffer" areas
(typically surrounding schools, day care centers, and the historic town plaza) in which no
commercial cannabis activities will be permitted irrespective whether that buffer area includes C
and/or MX zoned parcels. We note that there are two versions of the proposed ordinance that the
City Council may consider; one as submitted to the planning commission; and the second
incorporating the Planning Commission recommendations for a more expansive buffer zone around
the historic town plaza.

Adoption of the ordinance does not approve the establishment of any commercial
cannabis business. Adoption of the ordinance simply identifies the areas in the City in which such
businesses may, at some future time, be permitted after having successfully undertaken and
completed extensive review and permitting processes. There are 408 parcels in the MX zone and
311 parcels in the C zone. These zoning districts are generally clustered around Highway 12. Any
of these parcels are theoretical candidates for the establishment of such businesses (except that the
one store-front retail business must be located in the C zoning district). No applications are
currently at issue. Adoption of the ordinance simply identifies the areas in the City in which such
businesses may, at some future time, be permitted after having successfully undertaken and
completed extensive review and permitting processes.

Mayor Harrington's Residence

Mayor Harrington's owns and resides in a home near downtown Sonoma. Mayor
Harrington's interest in her home is in excess of $2,000,00. Mayor Harrington's residence is located
within 500 feet of portions of two parcels zoned MX; and more than 500 feet but less than 1,000
feet from 4 parcels zoned MX. Her residence is located more than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet
from a number of parcels zoned commercial, the C Zone. Under the proposed version of the
ordinance submitted to the planning commission, most of those parcels are located within a buffer

File No. A-19-126
Page No. 3

area. Under other version of the proposed ordinance, recommended by the Planning Commission,
all of the commercially zoned (C) parcels located within 1,000 feet of her residence would be
located in a buffer area where no cannabis related businesses would be allowed.

Except as noted below, Mayor Harrington's neighborhood is dominated by single-family
homes. It is located a block and a half from the City's Plaza, the center of the City. This plaza is the
most heavily patronized and trafficked City area in terms of vehicles, visitors and pedestrians.
Mayor Harrington's residence is located about two blocks from State Highway 12, which traverses
through the heart of the City and forms the southern boundary of the Plaza. Surrounding the Plaza
are multiple retail stores, restaurants, entertainment venues, service providers and wine tasting
shops. During particular events and periods of the year, parking in and around the Plaza is
problematic, sometimes spilling into the neighboring residential areas, including into the vicinity of
Mayor Harrington's residence.²

Almost directly across the street and to the north of Mayor Harrington's residence is the
Sonoma Community Center. According to the Community Center's 2018 Annual Report,
approximately 30,000 people used the Center in 2018. It is open 7 days a week, with events
occurring almost every day. The users and patrons of the Community Center generate noise,
vehicular traffic and parking demands impacting the subject property and its immediate environs.

Additionally, across the street from Mayor Harrington's residence, and four parcels to the
west, is an operating church whose parishioners and staff drive, park, and walk in the immediate
vicinity of Mayor Harrington's residence. There are no parking spaces available on the church
property.

Mayor Harrington's Law Practice and Office

Mayor Harrington is an attorney who practices in the City. Her law office is a shared office-
space with another attorney, in a commercial office building. Mayor Harrington's office-mate has a
one-year lease with the owner of the property. Under their shared office space arrangement, Mayor
Harrington pays her office-mate 50 percent of the officemate's rental obligation, minus 50 percent
of the cost of the phone service. Her practice focus is elder abuse, estate planning, and trusts. Her
arrangement may be terminated at any time by either party without advanced notice.

Her office is located in a MX-zoned parcel. It is located in a heavily traveled area, that can
receive an average of as much as 13,300 cars trips per day. Immediately to the east of the property
are large residentially zoned areas. Immediately to the west and across State Highway 12 are MX-
zoned parcels developed with residences, offices and some commercial users. Immediately to the
north of the property are MX-zoned parcels developed with other offices. And immediately south of
the property is the proposed buffer area in which all commercial cannabis businesses are precluded.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his
or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A

² On July 4, it is estimated that over 10,000 people visit Sonoma to witness its annual Fourth of July parade
that marches around the Plaza.

File No. A-19-126
Page No. 4

public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of the Act if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public
official's interests, including the following:

• A business entity interest, where an official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or
more in a business entity (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

• A real property interest, including a leasehold or option to acquire an interest, where an
official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b), 82033) An
individual's interest includes a pro rata share of interests in real property of any business
entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly, or
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033).

• An interest in a source of income, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or
promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

Mayor Harrington has a financial interest in her law practice, and any clients that are a
source of income to her within 12 months prior to the decision²; and she has a financial interest in
her residence. A real property interest includes a leasehold interest in real property. (Section 82033.)
However, Regulation 18233 provides that a periodic tenancy of one month or less is excluded from
the Act's definition of "interest in real property" and "leasehold interest." Therefore, because
Mayor Harrington rents her office on a month-to-month basis, she does not have a financial interest
in this property that could give rise to a conflict of interest.⁴

The standard for determining foreseeability set forth in Regulation 18701 differs depending
on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and a financial effect need not be likely
to be considered reasonably foreseeable. Here, neither Mayor Harrington's real property interest nor
business entity is explicitly involved in the decision, as neither is a named party to the decision nor
the subject of the decision. Therefore, the applicable standard is whether the financial effect can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical (Ibid.) If the financial
result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's
control, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

Regulation 18701(b)(1), applicable to your facts, provides that in determining whether a
governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a financial interest the
analysis will also consider "[t]he extent to which the occurrence of the financial effect is contingent
upon intervening events, not including future governmental decisions by the official's agency, or
any other agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the official's agency."

² You have not provided any information regarding clients, therefore, we do not further analyze the potential
financial effects (if any) on clients of the practice. If a client is implicated by the decision, Mayor Harrington may wish
to seek additional advice.

⁴ We reach this conclusion based on the limited facts provided, including the fact that the arrangement Mayor
Harrington has with her officemate regarding her tenancy is informal, and she has stated that the lease may be
terminated at any time by either party.

File No. A-19-126
Page No. 5

Residence:

Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standard for real property interests, which
varies depending on the proximity of the decision's impacts to the official's property interest.
Regulation 18702.2(a)(7) provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest is
material whenever the governmental decision involves property located 500 feet or less from the
property line of the parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not
have any measurable impact on the official's parcel.

The facts presented indicate that decision at issue proposes to allow a limited number of
cannabis businesses to be potentially located in already existing, heavily trafficked and utilized,
commercial and mixed use zones. The proposed ordinance allows no more than one store-front
retail dispensary to be located only in the City's C zoning district. It would also allow for one
non-store front retail dispensary, one testing laboratory and one non-volatile manufacturing
business to be conditionally permitted in the C zoning district and the MX zoning district. At most,
this ordinance would allow a total of four cannabis related business to operate within commercial
and mixed zones in the City, with impacts that are similar to other similar commercially
allowed uses.

The ordinance does not approve the establishment or specific location of any commercial
cannabis business. That is a separate extensive review and permitting processes. Due to the limited
scope of the changes proposed to the allowed uses in existing commercial and mix uses zones, and
the fact that any business seeking to operate within those zones would need to go through a separate
permitting process, it is not reasonably foreseeable the proposed zoning ordinance decision before
the City Council will have a material financial effect on the Mayor Harrington's residential real
property. Therefore, Mayor Harrington's interest in her residence would not preclude her from
participating in or influencing governmental decision-making pertinent to the proposed zoning
ordinance changes regarding the allowable location of future commercial cannabis businesses in the
City.

Business:

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides the materiality standard for business entities that are not
explicitly involved in a governmental decision. It states the financial effect is material if a prudent
person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's
financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business entity.
Examples of decisions that may be applicable include those that:

(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an
activity in which the business entity is engaged;

(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the
business entity is engaged;

(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business
entity supplies;

File No. A-19-126
Page No. 6

(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as
redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may
affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business
entity receives;

(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other
project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws
from the location of the project; or

(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the
business entity.

Mayor Harrington's practice focuses on elder abuse, estate planning, and trusts. The facts
presented would not cause a prudent person to find the decision regarding the allowance of
commercial cannabis businesses in the already zoned commercial or mixed use areas would
foreseeably contribute to a change in the value of this type of law practice. Therefore, it is not
reasonably foreseeable that a decision regarding the proposed zoning ordinance will materially
affect the Mayor Harrington's interest in her business. As noted above, no clients or customers were
identified for analysis under the source of income materiality criteria in Regulation 18702.3.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:aja

sections.question:
Does Mayor Amy Harrington's interest in her residential real property, rental office space, 
and law practice, preclude her from participating in or influencing governmental decision-making 
pertinent to the proposed zoning ordinance to allow for a limited number of commercial cannabis 
businesses in the commercial or mixed use zones?

Council will have a material financial effect on Mayor Harrington's residential real property or law 
practice. Because Mayor Harrington rents her office on a month-to-month basis, she does not have 
a financial interest in this property under the Act.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his 
or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A 
2 On July 4, it is estimated that over I 0,000 people visit Sono~a to witness its annual Fourth of July parade 
that marches around the Plaza.  
public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of the Act if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public 
official's interests, including the following: 
• 
A business entity interest, where an official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or 
more in a business entity (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). 
• 
A real property interest, including a leasehold or option to acquire an interest, where an 
official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b), 82033) An 
individual's interest includes a pro rata share of interests in real property of any business 
entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly, or 
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033). 
• 
An interest in a source of income, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or 
promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
Mayor Harrington has a financial interest in her law practice, and any clients that are a 
source of income to her within 12 months prior to the decision3; and she has a financial interest in 
her residence. A real property interest includes a leasehold interest in real property. (Section 82033.) 
However, Regulation 18233 provides that a periodic tenancy of one month or less is excluded from 
the Act's definition of "interest in real property" and "leasehold interest." Therefore, because 
Mayor Harrington rents her office on a month-to-month basis, she does not have a financial interest 
in this property that could give rise to a conflict of interest. 4 
The standard for determining foreseeability set forth in Regulation 18701 differs depending 
on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and a financial effect need not be likely 
to be considered reasonably forese able. Here, neither Mayor Harrington's real property interest nor 
business entity is explicitly involved' in the decision, as neither is a named party to the decision nor 
the subject of the decision. Therefore, the applicable standard is whether the financial effect can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical (Ibid.) If the financial 
result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's 
control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. 
Regulation 18701(b)(l), applicable to your facts, provides that in determining whether a 
governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a financial interest the 
analysis will also consider "[t]he extent to which the occurrence of the financial effect is contingent 
upon intervening events, not including future governmental decisions by the official's agency, or 
any other agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the official's agency." 
3 You have not provided any information regarding clients, therefore, we do not further analyze the potential 
financial effects (if any) on clients of the practice. If a client is implicated by the decision, Mayor Harrington may wish 
to seek additional advice. 
4 We reach this conclusion based on the limited facts provided, including the fact that the arrangement Mayor 
Harrington has with her officemate regarding her tenancy is informal, and she has stated that the lease may be 
terminated at any time by either party.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Mayor Amy Harrington's interest in her residential real property, rental office space, 
and law practice, preclude her from participating in or influencing governmental decision-making 
pertinent to the proposed zoning ordinance to allow for a limited number of commercial cannabis 
businesses in the commercial or mixed use zones?

CONCLUSION: No. It is not reasonably foreseeable the· proposed zoning ordinance decision before the City 
Council will have a material financial effect on Mayor Harrington's residential real property or law 
practice. Because Mayor Harrington rents her office on a month-to-month basis, she does not have 
a financial interest in this property under the Act.
